---
title: "TAMBAPANNI POSITIONED: A Strategic Framework for State Building and Macroeconomic Opportunity"
subtitle: "A Strategic Framework for State Building and Macroeconomic Opportunity"
date: 2026-03-01
author:
  - name: Ananthaneshan Elampoornan
    orcid: 0009-0004-9181-2270
google-scholar: true
citation:
  type: article
  container-title: "Tambapanni White Paper Series"
  issued: 2026-03
  url: https://tambapanni.github.io/papers/positioned.html
  pdf-url: https://tambapanni.github.io/papers/pdf/2. Tambapanni_Positioned.pdf
  doi: 10.5072/zenodo.471080
categories: [political-economy, state-building, institutional-reform, sri-lanka]
abstract: |
  For most of recorded history, the island known today as Sri Lanka sat at the centre of the world's most productive trading system. Arab navigators called it Serendib. Roman merchants knew it as Taprobane. Every great maritime power that needed to move goods between the Persian Gulf and the South China Sea passed through its waters. The 19th and 20th centuries interrupted that centrality. The 21st century is restoring it. A macroeconomic window is opening that small island economies at maritime trade intersections have historically used to leap forward. China's manufacturing overcapacity, shifting global supply chain architecture, and the Indian Ocean's emergence as the dominant trade corridor of the 21st century create conditions that are structurally favourable to Tambapanni, the island known today as Sri Lanka. References to the International Monetary Fund (IMF) and World Bank data throughout this paper draw on the October 2024 World Economic Outlook and 2024 World Development Indicators respectively.
  
  The constraint is not geography, talent, or capital availability. The constraint is institutional. A state built on ethnic and religious patronage cannot negotiate binding long-term infrastructure contracts, cannot attract the regulatory environment that global technology and financial services clients require, and cannot make credible commitments to foreign partners without those commitments being reversed by the next electoral cycle.
  
  This paper makes three arguments. First, the window is real and time-limited: the macroeconomic conditions that enabled Singapore, Taiwan, South Korea, Estonia, Rwanda, and Mauritius to leap forward each operated within a specific temporal window. Second, capturing the window requires not infrastructure spending but institutional reform: specifically the construction of an impartial state apparatus prior to ethnicity and religion in its founding logic. Third, the agent of that reform is not the state itself but a coalition of actors who share a forward-looking myth of national identity: prosperity as the shared telos, religion and language as personal rather than civic affairs.
---

## Part 2 of the Tambapanni Series

**Status:** Complete · v1.15 · March 2026

**Companion to:** [Tambapanni: A History Without Borders](history.qmd)

### Abstract

For most of recorded history, the island known today as Sri Lanka sat at the centre of the world's most productive trading system. Arab navigators called it Serendib. Roman merchants knew it as Taprobane. Every great maritime power that needed to move goods between the Persian Gulf and the South China Sea passed through its waters. The 19th and 20th centuries interrupted that centrality. The 21st century is restoring it. A macroeconomic window is opening that small island economies at maritime trade intersections have historically used to leap forward. China's manufacturing overcapacity, shifting global supply chain architecture, and the Indian Ocean's emergence as the dominant trade corridor of the 21st century create conditions that are structurally favourable to Tambapanni, the island known today as Sri Lanka. References to the International Monetary Fund (IMF) and World Bank data throughout this paper draw on the October 2024 World Economic Outlook and 2024 World Development Indicators respectively.

The constraint is not geography, talent, or capital availability. The constraint is institutional. A state built on ethnic and religious patronage cannot negotiate binding long-term infrastructure contracts, cannot attract the regulatory environment that global technology and financial services clients require, and cannot make credible commitments to foreign partners without those commitments being reversed by the next electoral cycle.

This paper makes three arguments. First, the window is real and time-limited: the macroeconomic conditions that enabled Singapore, Taiwan, South Korea, Estonia, Rwanda, and Mauritius to leap forward each operated within a specific temporal window. Second, capturing the window requires not infrastructure spending but institutional reform: specifically the construction of an impartial state apparatus prior to ethnicity and religion in its founding logic. Third, the agent of that reform is not the state itself but a coalition of actors who share a forward-looking myth of national identity: prosperity as the shared telos, religion and language as personal rather than civic affairs.

### Key Sections

- **China\'s Manufacturing Overcapacity**
- **The Indian Ocean Positioning**
- **The Comparisons: What the Window Looked Like Elsewhere**
- **Ia. Comparative Advantage, Not Competitive Advantage**
- **The Distinction**
- **Tambapanni\'s Comparative Advantages Are Structural and Unearned**
- **Where Tambapanni Has Been Looking Instead**
- **The Correct Frame: Converting Comparative Into Competitive**

### Frameworks Applied

- **Fukuyama** — Institutional sequencing: the impartial state must precede democratic accountability
- **Mahbubani** — The reader concludes, not the author; intelligence shared, not verdicts delivered
- **Dellanna** — Ergodicity, absorbing barriers, the power law trap for the majority
- **Ackoff** — Reactivism (recovering an idealised past) is the planning error

### Downloads

[Download PDF](pdf/2.%20Tambapanni_Positioned.pdf){.btn .btn-primary} [DOI: 10.5072/zenodo.471080](https://doi.org/10.5072/zenodo.471080){.btn .btn-outline-secondary}

### Citation

Elampoornan, A. (2026). Tambapanni Positioned: A Strategic Framework for State Building and Macroeconomic Opportunity, v1.15. orcid.org/0009-0004-9181-2270

---

[← Back to series overview](../index.qmd)
